nodes.name: Music Genres & Origins
nodes.description: Musical genres and their geographic and cultural roots

items.prompt: Which genre originated in the Mississippi Delta from African American spirituals and work songs, featuring a distinctive 12-bar chord progression?
items.explanation:
Originated in the Mississippi Delta region, late 19th century
Rooted in African American spirituals, work songs, field hollers, and chants
Standard 12-bar chord progression (I–I–I–I–IV–IV–I–I–V–IV–I–V) became the genre’s harmonic backbone
Robert Johnson’s 'Devil at the crossroads' legend is one of music’s most enduring myths
Muddy Waters electrified the sound in Chicago in the 1940s–1950s, bridging acoustic Delta style and rock and roll
B.B. King’s guitar 'Lucille' became the most famous named instrument in the genre
Direct ancestor of rock and roll, R&B, and virtually all modern popular music

items.prompt: Which genre was born in New Orleans in the early 1900s from a blend of ragtime, brass band marches, and field hollers, with improvisation as its defining feature?
items.explanation:
Born in New Orleans, early 1900s, from a melting pot of African, Caribbean, and European musical traditions
Blends ragtime syncopation, brass band marches, and vocal traditions into an improvisation-driven form
Louis Armstrong and King Oliver were early pioneers; Duke Ellington expanded it into orchestral composition
Miles Davis reshaped the genre at least three times: cool (1950s), modal (*Kind of Blue*, 1959), fusion (1970s)
Sub-genres include bebop (Parker, Gillespie), cool jazz (Chet Baker), free jazz (Ornette Coleman), and fusion
The word’s origin is debated — possibly from slang meaning energy or vigor
First major American art form to gain worldwide recognition

items.prompt: Which genre originated in Jamaica in the late 1960s, evolved from ska and rocksteady, and is characterized by an off-beat rhythmic pattern called the 'skank'?
items.explanation:
Originated in Jamaica, late 1960s, evolving from ska (fast) through rocksteady (slower) to a laid-back tempo
Off-beat rhythm guitar pattern called the 'skank' is the genre’s sonic signature
Bob Marley became the genre’s global ambassador; *Exodus* (1977) named album of the century by *Time* magazine
Deeply tied to the Rastafari movement — themes of Jah, Zion, and repatriation to Africa
Toots and the Maytals' 'Do the Reggay' (1968) is the first known use of the word in a song title
Lee 'Scratch' Perry and King Tubby pioneered dub, a remix-based offshoot that influenced electronic music worldwide
UNESCO added Jamaican reggae music to its Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2018

items.prompt: Which genre emerged in the mid-1970s from CBGB in New York and the London club scene, defined by short, fast, three-chord songs and a DIY ethic?
items.explanation:
Emerged mid-1970s simultaneously in NYC (Ramones, Television, Patti Smith at CBGB) and London (Sex Pistols, The Clash)
Short, fast, aggressive songs — the Ramones' debut album (1976) had 14 tracks in under 30 minutes
DIY ethic: anyone can start a band, print a zine, book a show — no gatekeepers needed
Sex Pistols' *Never Mind the Bollocks* (1977) and its single 'God Save the Queen' were banned by the BBC
The Clash's *London Calling* (1979) is consistently ranked among the greatest albums ever made
Spawned sub-genres: hardcore (Black Flag), pop-punk (Green Day), post-punk (Joy Division)
Anti-establishment stance influenced fashion (safety pins, torn clothing) as much as music

items.prompt: Which genre was born at a back-to-school party at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue in the South Bronx on August 11, 1973, when a DJ extended drum breaks using two turntables?
items.explanation:
Born August 11, 1973, at a party hosted by DJ Kool Herc (Clive Campbell) at 1520 Sedgwick Ave, Bronx, NYC
Herc’s 'merry-go-round' technique — looping drum breaks on two turntables — created the genre’s foundation
Four elements: DJing, MCing (rapping), breakdancing (b-boying), and graffiti art
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five's 'The Message' (1982) was the first socially conscious hit
'Rapper's Delight' by the Sugarhill Gang (1979) was the first commercially successful single
Run-DMC's collaboration with Aerosmith on 'Walk This Way' (1986) broke the genre into MTV mainstream
Became the most consumed music genre in the U.S. by streaming volume in the late 2010s

items.prompt: Which genre has roots in Appalachian folk music and Southern string bands, with the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers as its first recording stars in the 1920s?
items.explanation:
Roots in Appalachian folk music, Southern gospel, and Celtic/British Isles ballad traditions
The Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers made the first major commercial recordings in Bristol, TN (1927) — the 'Big Bang of Country Music'
Grand Ole Opry began broadcasting from Nashville on WSM radio on November 28, 1925; still running weekly
Signature instruments: steel guitar, fiddle, banjo, and the 'twang' of a nasal vocal style
Hank Williams (1923–1953) defined the honky-tonk sound and died at 29 en route to a concert
Nashville became 'Music City' largely because of the Opry and the recording studios on Music Row
Sub-genres include bluegrass (Bill Monroe), outlaw country (Willie Nelson, Waylon Jennings), and country pop

items.prompt: Which genre blended samba rhythms with cool jazz harmonies in late-1950s Brazil, producing the internationally famous song 'The Girl from Ipanema'?
items.explanation:
Originated in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, late 1950s, blending samba’s syncopation with cool jazz harmony
João Gilberto’s whispery vocal style and fingerpicked guitar defined the genre’s intimate sound
Antonio Carlos Jobim composed many of the genre’s standards, including 'The Girl from Ipanema' and 'Corcovado'
'The Girl from Ipanema' (composed 1962, famous Getz/Gilberto recording released 1964) became the second-most-recorded song in history after 'Yesterday'
Stan Getz’s saxophone brought the sound to American audiences; Astrud Gilberto sang the English lyrics almost by accident
Name translates roughly to 'new trend' or 'new wave' in Brazilian Portuguese slang
Soft, syncopated guitar rhythm with understated vocals — the opposite of samba’s carnival energy

items.prompt: Which genre emerged in early-1970s New York City nightclubs, peaked with the *Saturday Night Fever* soundtrack in 1977, and was symbolically destroyed at a baseball stadium riot in Chicago?
items.explanation:
Emerged in early-1970s NYC clubs, rooted in funk, soul, and Philadelphia International Records' lush orchestral sound
Four-on-the-floor kick drum pattern at ~120 BPM became the genre’s rhythmic signature
Donna Summer, the 'Queen of Disco,' scored hits with 'Love to Love You Baby' (1975) and 'I Feel Love' (1977)
*Saturday Night Fever* soundtrack (1977) sold 40+ million copies; the Bee Gees dominated the era
Disco Demolition Night (July 12, 1979): DJ Steve Dahl blew up disco records at Comiskey Park in Chicago, causing a riot and a White Sox forfeit
Studio 54 in Manhattan became the era’s most famous nightclub (1977–1980)
Direct ancestor of house music — Frankie Knuckles in Chicago carried the sound forward after disco’s commercial collapse

items.prompt: Which genre emerged from the Seattle music scene in the late 1980s, fusing punk attitude with heavy guitar distortion, and was closely associated with Sub Pop Records and flannel fashion?
items.explanation:
Emerged in Seattle, late 1980s, fusing punk rock’s energy and attitude with heavy metal’s distorted guitars
Nirvana’s *Nevermind* (1991) and its single 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' made the genre a global phenomenon overnight
Pearl Jam, Soundgarden, Alice in Chains, and Mudhoney were the other key bands of the movement
Sub Pop Records (founded 1986 in Seattle by Bruce Pavitt and Jonathan Poneman) was the genre’s flagship label
Anti-fashion aesthetic: flannel shirts, ripped jeans, thrift-store clothing — a rejection of 1980s glam
Kurt Cobain’s death on April 5, 1994, is often cited as the symbolic end of the movement’s peak
Mudhoney vocalist Mark Arm is credited with first using the word 'grunge' to describe the Seattle sound in a 1981 fanzine letter

items.prompt: Which electronic genre originated in Detroit in the early–mid 1980s, created by three high school friends from Belleville, Michigan, and heavily influenced by Kraftwerk?
items.explanation:
Originated in Detroit, early–mid 1980s, combining Kraftwerk’s electronic precision with funk and post-industrial aesthetics
The 'Belleville Three' — Juan Atkins ('Godfather'), Derrick May ('Innovator'), Kevin Saunderson ('Elevator') — are the genre’s founders
Juan Atkins' group Cybotron released 'Techno City' (1984), one of the first uses of the term
Repetitive 4/4 beat, synthesizers, drum machines (especially the Roland TR-808 and TR-909)
Influenced by DJ Charles 'The Electrifying Mojo' Johnson’s eclectic late-night radio show on WGPR in Detroit
Neil Rushton licensed Detroit tracks for UK release in 1988, branding them 'techno' to distinguish from Chicago house
Berlin’s Love Parade (1989–2010) became the genre’s largest cultural event, drawing over a million attendees

items.prompt: Which genre’s name was coined in 1947 by Billboard journalist Jerry Wexler to replace the term 'race music,' combining gospel, jazz, and boogie-woogie into a new popular sound?
items.explanation:
Term coined by Billboard’s Jerry Wexler in 1947 to replace the offensive label 'race music'; appeared in chart name June 1949
Originated in African American communities, combining jazz instrumentation, gospel vocals, and boogie-woogie rhythms
Ray Charles blended gospel fervor with secular lyrics, essentially inventing soul music in the process
Sam Cooke, Ruth Brown ('Miss Rhythm'), and Fats Domino were early stars of the 1950s
Evolved into sub-genres: soul (1960s), funk (1970s), new jack swing (late 1980s), contemporary/modern R&B (1990s–present)
Motown Records (founded 1959 by Berry Gordy in Detroit) became the genre’s most successful label
The term’s meaning has shifted dramatically — 1940s R&B sounds nothing like 2020s R&B, but the label persists

items.prompt: Which genre emerged in the late 1960s–early 1970s in the English Midlands, pioneered by bands using extreme volume and distortion, with a name possibly inspired by a phrase in Steppenwolf’s 'Born to Be Wild'?
items.explanation:
Emerged late 1960s–early 1970s in the UK, primarily Birmingham and the English Midlands
Black Sabbath (formed 1968) are widely considered the genre’s originators — Tony Iommi’s detuned, heavy riffs set the template
Led Zeppelin and Deep Purple were the other two pillars of the early sound
Steppenwolf’s 'Born to Be Wild' (1968) contains the lyric 'heavy metal thunder' — the first use of the phrase in rock music
Characterized by distorted guitars, powerful drumming, dense bass-heavy sound, and often virtuosic solos
Sub-genres proliferated: thrash (Metallica), death metal (Death), black metal (Mayhem), doom metal (Candlemass)
The 'Big Four' of thrash metal: Metallica, Megadeth, Slayer, and Anthrax — all formed in the early 1980s
